name: Ellen Henrietta Swallow Richards
whatOpened: Founded sanitary chemistry and home economics - first US-wide water-quality standards and modern domestic hygiene movement.
short: Ellen Swallow Richards was the first woman to graduate from Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and a pioneer of sanitary chemistry and home economics.
long: Ellen Swallow Richards was born on December 3, 1842, in Dunstable, Massachusetts. From an early age, she showed a keen interest in science and nature, although opportunities for women in scientific education were extremely limited at the time. She earned her bachelor's degree in chemistry from Vassar College in 1870 and, making history, became the first woman admitted to MIT as a "special student." She graduated in 1873 with a B.S. in Chemistry, breaking barriers for women in higher education. 2 In 1876, Richards' efforts led to the creation of the first "Women's Laboratory" at MIT - a dedicated space where women could study chemistry, biology, mineralogy, and industrial chemistry. There she taught, mentored, and inspired many women, helping to open scientific education to women at a time when such opportunities were severely restricted. By 1884, MIT had established the first laboratory of sanitary chemistry in the United States, with Richards serving as an instructor - a position she held until her death in 1911. Her work focused on analyzing water, air, and food for quality and safety. In 1887, she led a landmark survey of Massachusetts inland waters, testing tens of thousands of samples for pollution. The findings from her water-quality survey contributed to the establishment of the first state water-quality standards in the United States and prompted construction of one of the first modern municipal sewage-treatment plants. Beyond public health, Richards applied her scientific knowledge to everyday life: she published influential works such as The Chemistry of Cooking and Cleaning and Air, Water and Food from a Sanitary Standpoint , effectively founding the discipline of home economics, promoting food safety, nutrition, sanitation, and efficient household management. Through her groundbreaking efforts, Richards made science accessible to women, created professional paths in public health and domestic science, and laid the foundation for modern environmental hygiene, nutrition standards, and the broader field of applied chemistry. Her legacy continues to inspire scientists, educators, and public health advocates.
sources: 1. Encyclopedia Britannica - "Ellen Swallow Richards." britannica.com 3
2. Women of the Hall - "Ellen Swallow Richards Biography." womenofthehall.org

name: Matilda Moldenhauer Brooks
whatOpened: Discovered methylene blue as an antidote to carbon - monoxide and cyanide poisoning - a breakthrough in toxicology and cell biology.
short: Matilda Moldenhauer Brooks (1888-1981) was an American biologist who demonstrated that the dye methylene blue can counteract cyanide and carbon - monoxide poisoning, making a major contribution to medical toxicology and cell biology.
long: Matilda Moldenhauer Brooks was born in 1888 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, daughter of Rudolph and Selma (Neuffer) Moldenhauer. She completed her early education, then earned a B.A. and M.A. at the University of Pittsburgh. In 1920 she received her Ph.D. in zoology from Harvard University with a thesis titled Quantitative studies on the respiration of Bacillus subtilis . 5 Shortly after earning her doctorate, Brooks began working for the United States Public Health Service (USPHS), where she was employed from 1920 until 1927. It was during this period that she worked on biological and cell - physiology research, often collaborating with her husband, Sumner Cushing Brooks, whom she had married in 1917. In 1927 Sumner Brooks was offered a faculty position at University of California, Berkeley. Because of the university's anti - nepotism rules at that time, Matilda was not given a paid professorship - instead she held a research - staff appointment without salary. Nonetheless, she continued her research there, publishing independent studies and collaborating with her husband for decades. During her years at Berkeley, Brooks investigated the "permeability of living cells" - that is, how molecules and ions penetrate and are exchanged by living cellular membranes. In 1932 she published a landmark paper in Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA), proposing that the dye Methylene blue can act as an antidote for cyanide and carbon - monoxide poisoning - based on her experiments on living cells under oxidative stress. This suggestion was groundbreaking: it connected fundamental cell - physiology research to real - world medicine and toxicology. Despite institutional constraints - lack of salary, reliance on small grants and personal savings - Brooks persisted. After her husband's death in 1948, she continued her scientific work, supported only by a modest stipend from Berkeley, small grants, and her own resources. Over her career she authored and co - authored important works - among them the monograph The Permeability of Living Cells (1941) with her husband - a seminal contribution to cell biology. 6 Brooks also spent summers and research periods at marine - biology stations (e.g. at Marine Biological Laboratory in Woods Hole, Massachusetts), which was common among biologists of her time, indicating her active involvement in broader biological research communities. She passed away in 1981 (likely in San Francisco), leaving a legacy as a committed and persistent scientist whose cell - biology research - especially on membrane permeability and methylene - blue antidote potential - opened important paths in toxicology and understanding of living cells.

name: Chien - Shiung Wu
location: Born in Liuhe, Taicang, Jiangsu province, China
whatOpened: Provided the first experimental proof that parity is violated in weak nuclear interactions - fundamentally changed understanding of symmetry in physics.
short: Chien - Shiung Wu was a Chinese - American experimental physicist whose 1956 "Wu experiment" definitively showed that weak interactions violate parity symmetry - a breakthrough that reshaped particle physics. She also contributed to the uranium - enrichment process during the Manhattan Project.
long: Chien - Shiung Wu was born on May 31, 1912, in Liuhe, in Taicang (Jiangsu province), China. From early on, she grew up in a family supportive of education - something uncommon for girls in her time. She studied physics at National Central University in Nanjing (graduating 1934) before moving to the United States in 1936 for graduate study at the University of California, Berkeley. In 1940 she earned her Ph.D. in physics at Berkeley under the mentoring of prominent physicists. 8 After her doctorate, Wu worked briefly as lecturer and then - with World War II underway - became involved in critical wartime research. She joined the Columbia University team (as part of the Manhattan Project) and helped develop the gaseous - diffusion process for separating uranium isotopes (U - 235 and U - 238), a key step in nuclear physics and atomic research. In 1956, acting on theoretical work by her colleagues Tsung - Dao Lee and Chen - Ning Yang, Wu conducted a decisive experiment with radioactive cobalt - 60 ("Wu experiment") and observed that beta particles emitted in weak nuclear decay had a preferred direction - thus demonstrating that the law of parity conservation fails in those interactions.This result upended a core assumption in physics and laid groundwork for modern particle theory. After the war, Wu continued her academic career at Columbia University. In 1957 she became Dupin Professor of Physics, later in 1972 Michael I. Pupin Professor, and remained there until her retirement in 1981. Her achievements brought many honors: she received the National Medal of Science (1975), and in 1978 she became the first recipient of the Wolf Prize in Physics for her experimental work. Chien - Shiung Wu died on February 16, 1997, in New York City. Her legacy lives on: she remains one of the most influential experimental physicists of the 20th century, celebrated for combining technical mastery with bold challenges to established scientific orthodoxy.

name: Nettie Maria Stevens
location: Born in Cavendish, Vermont, United States
whatOpened: Demonstrated that sex is determined by specific chromosomes (X and Y), providing first strong evidence that heredity - not environment - defines sex in many species.
short: Nettie Stevens was a pioneering American geneticist. In 1905 she published research showing that in mealworms (and later in other insects), the presence of a small "Y" chromosome - paired with a larger one - determined male sex, while two larger chromosomes produced female offspring. This was the first clear link between chromosomes and sex determination.
long: Nettie Maria Stevens was born on July 7, 1861, in Cavendish, Vermont. She grew up in a modest family; after early education she attended the State Normal School (now Westfield State College) in Massachusetts. Later, around the age 35, she entered Stanford University, earning a B.A. in 1899 and an M.A. in 1900. She then pursued graduate studies at Bryn Mawr College, receiving her Ph.D. in 1903. After earning her doctorate, Stevens remained at Bryn Mawr as a research fellow, then lecturer, and eventually as associate in experimental morphology. Her early research focused on the morphology and taxonomy of protozoa, but by the early 1900s she turned to cytology and embryology. In 1905, she conducted experiments on the mealworm Tenebrio molitor and discovered that male sperm carried either a large chromosome or a much smaller one - while female eggs carried only the large version. Observing that the offspring sex correlated with which chromosome the sperm carried, she concluded that sex was determined by specific chromosomes. 12 This discovery provided the first firm evidence that heredity - via chromosomes - determines sex, not environment or "temperament." Her 1905-1906 works have been recognized as foundational in cytogenetics and heredity science. Despite her groundbreaking work, Stevens faced the institutional biases common for women scientists at her time: she did not achieve a full professorial chair, but persisted in research. Nettie Stevens died on May 4, 1912 in Baltimore, Maryland, from breast cancer, at age 50. Although her life was short, she published dozens of papers and left a legacy: her work laid the groundwork for modern genetics and cytology, and our understanding of how sex is biologically determined.

2. «Nettie Maria Stevens (1861 - 1912)» - Embryo Project Encyclopedia. Embryo Project Encyclopedia

name: George Green
whatOpened: I ntroduced the concept of mathematical potential, formulated what is now known as Green's theorem and developed the foundations of modern potential theory and mathematical treatment of electricity and magnetism - widely used in physics and engineering.
short: George Green was an English mathematician and physicist, largely self - taught, whose 1828 work dramatically changed physics: his mathematical methods introduced the idea of potential and laid the groundwork for modern electromagnetism and potential theory.
long: George Green was born on 14 July 1793 in Sneinton (then a village, now part of Nottingham), England, the son of a prosperous miller. As a child he received only minimal formal education - about four terms at a local academy - before he was withdrawn to help in his father's bakery and later the family mill. 14 Despite his limited formal schooling and life as a miller, Green nourished a deep passion for mathematics. He studied on his own, likely using books available at the local library, and by 1828 - while still working at the mill - he privately published his seminal treatise An Essay on the Application of Mathematical Analysis to the Theories of Electricity and Magnetism . In this essay he introduced the notion of a "potential function," and formulated results that would later be known as Green's theorem - mathematical tools that allowed rigorous treatment of electric and magnetic fields. The publication caught the attention of a local patron, Sir Edward Bromhead, who encouraged Green and helped him gain recognition among mathematicians. With that support, at the age of ~40, Green entered Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge (in 1833). He graduated in 1837 (as Fourth Wrangler) and shortly afterwards was elected a fellow of the college. During his brief academic career, Green published additional influential works - on fluid equilibrium, on wave motion (sound, light), on hydrodynamics, and on reflection/refraction of waves - extending his methods beyond electromagnetism into broader areas of mathematical physics. Unfortunately, his health deteriorated, and in 1840 he returned to Nottingham. He died there on 31 May 1841. For much of the 19th century Green's contributions remained relatively obscure. Only after 1846, when William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin (Lord Kelvin) recognized their importance, did Green's work receive full appreciation. Over time his theorems and methods became foundational in electromagnetism, potential theory, hydrodynamics, acoustics, and later in quantum mechanics, engineering and many applied sciences. 15 Today, memorials (including a memorial stone in Westminster Abbey and a restored windmill - museum in Nottingham) honour George Green's legacy as one of the founders of mathematical physics.

name: Julius Robert von Mayer
whatOpened: One of the first to formulate the principle of conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics) - that energy (work, heat) is conserved - and early attempt to quantify the mechanical equivalent of heat.
short: Julius Robert von Mayer (1814-1878) was a German physician and physicist whose 1842 work laid down one of the earliest clear statements of energy conservation - the idea that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed.
long: Julius Robert von Mayer was born on 25 November 1814 in Heilbronn, Kingdom of Württemberg. He studied medicine - enrolling at the University of Tübingen, and later worked as a physician. In 1840 Mayer embarked as a ship's surgeon on a Dutch vessel bound for Java (East Indies). During this voyage, he observed that the venous blood of sailors in tropical climates appeared much redder than usual - leading him to reflect on the relationship between heat, metabolism, and chemical processes in living organisms. This insight contributed to his thinking about heat and energy conservation. After returning to Heilbronn, in 1842 Mayer published a paper "Bemerkungen über die Kräfte der unbelebten Natur" ("Remarks on the Forces of Inanimate Nature") in which he articulated a general law that what we now call conservation of energy: proposing that mechanical work and heat are interconvertible, and that total energy remains constant. He attempted a quantitative evaluation of the mechanical equivalent of heat - though his numerical result was inaccurate by modern standards, the conceptual leap was pivotal. 18 Mayer continued to work in medicine in Heilbronn while refining his ideas; his writings were initially largely ignored due to poor presentation and lack of institutional connections. Late recognition began to come: in later years some scientists acknowledged his priority in formulating energy conservation. Mayer died on 20 March 1878 in Heilbronn. Although during his lifetime his ideas were underappreciated, today Julius Robert von Mayer is regarded as one of the founding fathers of thermodynamics and a pioneer in understanding the fundamental nature of energy transformation and conservation - ideas that now underpin all of modern physics and engineering.

name: Elizabeth Langdon Williams
whatOpened: Her precise calculations for Percival Lowell's "Planet X" project laid groundwork that helped locate what later became recognized as Pluto.
short: Elizabeth Langdon Williams (1879-1981) - American human computer and astronomer. Her complex orbit - calculations under Lowell Observatory's Planet X search contributed to the eventual discovery of Pluto.
long: Elizabeth Langdon Williams was born on February 8, 1879, in Putnam, Connecticut, United States. She graduated in 1903 from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) with a degree in physics - among the earliest women to graduate from MIT. In 1905, she was hired by astronomer Percival Lowell to work in his Boston office, initially editing publications. Soon she became a "human computer": performing the laborious manual calculations needed for Lowell's hypothesized 21 "Planet X" - a yet - undiscovered planet believed to perturb the orbits of Neptune and Uranus. From about 1910, Williams served as head human computer for the Planet X program, calculating possible orbital parameters and sky positions for the unknown planet. After Lowell's death in 1916, the search was paused; but decades later - when the search resumed - astronomers used predictions stemming from her earlier calculations, which helped pinpoint the object that would be named Pluto in 1930. Williams moved in 1919 from Boston to the observatory in Flagstaff, Arizona, where she handled further calculations and administrative correspondence at the Lowell Observatory. In 1922 she married astronomer George Hall Hamilton; shortly after their marriage, both were dismissed from Lowell Observatory - as employing married women was then deemed inappropriate. The couple then worked for a time at a station of Harvard College Observatory in Mandeville, Jamaica. After her husband's death in 1935, Williams retired and eventually moved to New Hampshire, where she lived until her death in 1981 in Enfield, at the age of 101. Although her contributions were long underappreciated - overshadowed by social norms and the work of others - today Elizabeth Langdon Williams is recognized as an early - and critical - female astronomer whose calculations played a key role in one of the major discoveries of the Solar System.

name: Ida Eva Noddack (née Tacke)
location: Lackhausen (today part of Wesel), Rhine Province, German Empire
whatOpened: Co - discovered the chemical element Rhenium and was among the first to propose the possibility of nuclear fission.
short: Ida Noddack (1896-1978) was a German chemist who - together with her colleagues - discovered rhenium in 1925, and in 1934 became the first scientist to publicly hypothesize that atomic nuclei, when bombarded with neutrons, might split - a conceptual precursor to nuclear fission.
long: Ida Eva Tacke was born on 25 February 1896 in Lackhausen (now part of Wesel), in the Rhineland region of Germany. Despite societal norms that limited women's roles in science, she pursued higher education and in 1915 entered the Technical University of Berlin - Charlottenburg - at a time when few German women studied chemistry. She completed her diploma and in 1921 obtained her doctorate, with a dissertation on high aliphatic fatty - acid anhydrides. By 1925, at the Physico - Technical Research Agency in Berlin, she collaborated with Walter Noddack (her future husband) and Otto Berg, and together they discovered the element rhenium (atomic number 75), named after the Rhine region. They also claimed discovery of a second element, "masurium" (atomic number 43), which later proved to be incorrect, as Technetium was only identified in 1937. In 1934, Noddack published a paper proposing that uranium nuclei, when bombarded with neutrons, might split - a conceptual precursor to nuclear fission. Her idea was ignored at the time but later confirmed by experiments in 1938-1939. 25 She received several honors during her career, including the Liebig Commemorative Medal (1931), the Scheele Medal, the Great Cross of Merit of Germany (1966), and an honorary doctorate. Ida Noddack continued her scientific work until retirement and died on 24 September 1978 in Bad Neuenahr, Germany. Her discovery of rhenium and her early insight on nuclear fission left a lasting legacy in chemistry and nuclear physics.

name: Florence Bascom
whatOpened: Showed that crystalline Appalachian rocks could be systematically studied
short: Florence Bascom (1862-1945) was a pioneering American geologist - the first woman to earn a Ph.D. in geology from an American university (Johns Hopkins University, 1893), and the first woman to be hired by the United States Geological Survey (USGS). Her work on petrography, crystalline rocks, and geological mapping of the Appalachian/Piedmont region laid foundations for modern U.S. regional geology and trained many early women geologists.
long: Florence Bascom was born on 14 July 1862 in Williamstown, Massachusetts, USA. Her family supported women's education: her father, John Bascom, was a professor and later university president, and her mother, Emma Curtiss Bascom, was a teacher and women's - rights advocate. 27 Bascom attended the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where she earned a Bachelor of Arts in 1882 and a Bachelor of Science in 1884, followed by a Master's in geology in 1887. She then moved to Johns Hopkins University, and in 1893 became the first woman to earn a Ph.D. from that institution - and only the second woman in the United States to earn a Ph.D. in geology. After completing her education, Bascom taught at several institutions before in 1895 founding the Geology Department at Bryn Mawr College (a women's college). She combined teaching with active geological research and fieldwork. In 1896 Bascom became the first woman hired by USGS. Her geological work focused on petrography, crystallography, and mapping: she studied crystalline rock formations in the Appalachian Piedmont region (Pennsylvania, Maryland, New Jersey) - an effort that produced influential USGS folios and bulletins. Her Ph.D. dissertation reinterpreted certain metamorphic rocks: she showed petrographically that rocks earlier classified as sediments were in fact metamorphosed lava flows - a significant correction in geological understanding. Over her long career, Bascom not only produced important geological mappings and petrographic classifications, but also trained many women geologists, thereby helping open geology as a profession to women. She also took part in scientific societies: she was among the first women fellows of the Geological Society of America (elected 1894), later became its councillor (1924) and vice - president (1930) - roles almost never held by women at the time. Florence Bascom continued her academic and research work until her retirement (official from Bryn Mawr in 1928), but remained active in USGS assignments until the 1930s. 28 She passed away on 18 June 1945 in Williamstown, Massachusetts. Her legacy - in terms of petrography, geological mapping, recognition of crystalline - rock geology, and inclusion of women in geosciences - remains a cornerstone of American geology.

name: Josephine Garis Cochran
whatOpened: Introduced the first practical water - pressure dishwashing machine that became the basis for modern dishwashers.
short: Josephine Cochran (1839-1913) was an American inventor who created and built the first commercially successful dishwasher.
long: Josephine Garis was born on March 8, 1839 (some sources list 1841) in Ashtabula County, Ohio, USA. In 1858 she married William Apperson Cochran and later settled in Shelbyville, Illinois. After her husband's death in 1883 left her in debt, Cochran sought a practical solution to a common household problem: fine china used in her house was often chipped during hand washing. Motivated by that, she began designing a mechanical dishwasher - despite having no formal mechanical training - and collaborated with a mechanic, George Butters, to build a prototype. Her design differed from previous attempts: dishes were placed in wire compartments inside a wheel, which sat flat in a copper boiler; a motor rotated the wheel while hot, soapy water was pumped from the bottom - using water pressure rather than scrubbers. On 28 December 1886, she was granted U.S. Patent No. 355,139 for her "dish - washing machine." To bring her invention to life, she founded the company Garis - Cochran Dish - Washing Machine Company, which manufactured and marketed her machines. In 1893 she exhibited her dishwasher at the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago - nine of her machines were installed in restaurants and pavilions - and won a prize for "best mechanical construction, durability and adaptation to its line of work." 31 Because early home plumbing and costs limited private use, her dishwashers were mainly purchased by hotels, restaurants and institutions. Over time her company evolved (later renamed Cochran's Crescent Washing Machine Company) and eventually was acquired by the company that became KitchenAid, so her basic design influenced the modern dishwasher. Josephine Cochran continued managing her business and refining her invention until her death on August 3, 1913 in Chicago, Illinois. Her invention - the first practical dishwasher - laid the foundation for a household appliance that would eventually become commonplace worldwide; her design principles (wire racks, water pressure washing) persist in modern dishwashers.

name: Taqi al-Din
whatOpened: Established a modern observatory in Istanbul and introduced decimal - fraction based astronomical calculations.
short: Taqī al - Dīn was a prominent Ottoman astronomer and polymath who founded the first major observatory in Istanbul and pioneered the use of decimal fractions in trigonometric and astronomical computations.
long: Taqī al - Dīn was born on 14 June 1526 in Damascus (then part of the Ottoman Empire). He studied traditional religious sciences and Arabic, then expanded his education into mathematical sciences under notable scholars in Damascus and Egypt, including training in astronomy, mathematics, and optics. Around 1550 he moved to Istanbul with his father, later spending about two decades in Egypt, where he continued both religious and scientific work . In 1570 33 he returned to Istanbul, and in 1571 he was appointed as the chief astronomer (Müneccimbāşı) under Sultan Selim II. Encouraged by the patronage of Sultan Murad III, Taqī al - Dīn founded the Istanbul Observatory (Dār al - Rasad al - Jadīd), completed in 1579, located on a height overlooking Tophane. Under his direction, the observatory housed a library, workshop for instrument making, and a staff of astronomers, clerks, and assistants. Taqī al - Dīn reproduced traditional instruments and invented new observational tools, including a sextant - like instrument (mushabbaha bi - 'l manātiq), and mechanical clocks for astronomical observations - among the first in the Islamic world. He also wrote works on mechanics, optics, mathematics, and natural philosophy. In his astronomical work - notably the zij titled Sidrat Muntahā al - Afkār fī Malakūt al - Falak al - Dawwār - he combined observations from Egypt and Istanbul, improved trigonometric calculations (including computing sin 1° via a novel method), and compiled tables using decimal fractions instead of the traditional sexagesimal system. He used a method of "three observation points" for determining solar parameters, and applied stars near the ecliptic (rather than the Moon) for stellar longitude/latitude calculations - yielding results more precise than contemporaries like Nicolaus Copernicus or Tycho Brahe in some respects. Besides astronomy, he authored works on mechanics - including a treatise describing water - raising machines, automatic clocks, hydraulics and mechanical devices. However, the observatory existed only a short time: it was demolished on 22 January 1580 due to political and religious pressures. 34 After the destruction, although Taqī al - Dīn continued his intellectual work, his astronomical programme remained incomplete. He died in 1585 in Istanbul. Despite the loss of many manuscripts and the brief life of his observatory, Taqī al - Dīn's work represents a high point of Ottoman scientific achievement in the 16th century. His use of mechanical clocks for astronomy, decimal - based calculations, and improved trigonometric tables contributed significantly to the development of observational astronomy and mathematical methods.
sources: 1. Biographical Encyclopedia of Astronomers, entry on Taqī al - Dīn ISMI
2. "Taqi al - Din ibn Ma'ruf: A Bio - Bibliographical Essay", MuslimHeritage.com Muslim Heritage+1

name: Ibn al-Shatir
whatOpened: Developed new planetary models without problematic constructs of Ptolemaic astronomy, paving way to more accurate celestial predictions.
short: Ibn al - Shatir was a Syrian astronomer, mathematician and engineer, who reworked Ptolemaic astronomy: he designed mathematical models of the Sun, Moon and planets that better matched observations, and built astronomical instruments - among the most accomplished astronomers of medieval Islam.
long: Ibn al - Shatir was born around 1304 in Damascus. After his father's death when he was young, he was raised by his grandfather, who taught him craftsmanship including instrument - making - a skill that later proved vital for his astronomical work. He studied astronomy and mathematics in major centers of learning (including Cairo and Alexandria), absorbing both Islamic and Greek astronomical traditions. Later he returned to Damascus and was appointed 36 muwaqqit(timekeeper) at the Umayyad Mosque - a role that required precise determination of prayer times, which in turn demanded accurate astronomical observations and instruments. As part of his duties and personal research, Ibn al - Shatir crafted sundials, astrolabes, and other observational tools; one of his most famous achievements was the sundial installed on the minaret of the Umayyad Mosque around 1371/72. But his most enduring legacy stems from his theoretical work. In his treatise Nihāyat al - suʾl fī taṣḥīḥ al - uṣūl ("The Final Quest Concerning the Rectification of Principles") Ibn al - Shatir proposed new mathematical models for planetary motions: his models eliminated the Ptolemaic equant and deferent/epicycle problems by using clever combinations of epicycles (including what later became known as the "Tusi couple" and Urdi lemmas), achieving planetary and lunar positions in better agreement with observation. In particular he resolved longstanding issues with the orbits of the Moon and Mercury - problems that had troubled earlier astronomers - and introduced trigonometric and computational refinements that improved the precision of celestial predictions. His models are now recognized by historians of astronomy as mathematically equivalent (though conceptually different) to those later used by Nicolaus Copernicus - suggesting that medieval Islamic astronomy under Ibn al - Shatir may have influenced European developments centuries later. Ibn al - Shatir died around 1375 in Damascus. Despite the destruction or loss of many original manuscripts and instruments over time, his astronomical theory and innovations remain a landmark of medieval astronomy and an important, 37 though often under - acknowledged, link in the history of science bridging Islamic and European traditions.

name: Annie Jean Easley
whatOpened: : Created computer code for rocket - stage and energy - conversion systems, laying groundwork for modern space and energy tech.
short: Annie Easley (1933-2011) was an American mathematician and computer scientist whose programming work at NACA/NASA helped develop rocket - stage software and early alternative - energy systems, breaking racial and gender barriers in STEM.
long: Annie Jean Easley was born on 23 April 1933 in Birmingham, Alabama. In 1955 she responded to a newspaper ad and was hired as a "human computer" by the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics (NACA), predecessor of NASA, at the Lewis Research Center in Cleveland, Ohio - she became one of only four African - American employees among roughly 2,500 staff at that time. 39 Her earliest work involved performing manual calculations and simulations for the Plum Brook Reactor Facility. As computing technology advanced and replaced human "computers," Easley adapted: she learned programming and became a computer scientist, mastering languages such as FORTRAN, to develop and implement code for multiple NASA projects. Among her significant contributions was her work on the software for the Centaur rocket upper stage - a powerful rocket stage that enabled heavy - payload satellites and space probes to be launched. She also contributed to studies of energy - conversion systems, alternative energy, and battery - storage technologies for early hybrid - power research. In the 1970s, while working full - time at NASA, Easley returned to school and earned a B.S. in Mathematics from Cleveland State University in 1977. Beyond her technical contributions, Annie Easley became a pioneer and advocate: she worked as an Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO) counselor at NASA - helping address issues of gender, race, and age discrimination. She also participated in school outreach, tutoring, and was active in inspiring under - represented students to pursue STEM careers. After a 34 - year career, she retired in 1989. Annie Easley passed away on 25 June 2011 in Cleveland, Ohio. Despite social and institutional barriers, Annie Easley's work laid important technical foundations for rocketry and renewable - energy research, and her personal example helped pave the way for greater inclusion of women and minorities in STEM fields.

name: Ibn Yunus
whatOpened: Created highly precise astronomical tables improving on Ptolemaic data for solar, lunar, and planetary motion.
short: Ibn Yunus was a leading medieval Egyptian astronomer and mathematician whose astronomical tables and observations underpinned centuries - long advances in celestial mechanics and timekeeping.
long: Ibn Yunus was born around 950 in the city of Fustat (ancient Egypt). His family was scholarly: his father was a noted historian. Little is known about his early education. What is known - from medieval and modern sources - is that he became active as an astronomer during the period when Egypt was under the rule of the Fatimid Caliphate. Under patronage of the Fatimid rulers, he gained access to instruments and an observatory near Cairo (on the Muqattam hill) where he could conduct observations. 42 From about 977 to 1003, he carried out systematic observations of the Sun, Moon, and planets - gathering data on eclipses, conjunctions, and celestial positions. His magnum opus, a large astronomical handbook known as al - Zīj al - Ḥākimī al - kabīr ("Great Ḥākimī Zij"), was completed around 1007. This work contained extensive tables - including solar, lunar, and planetary positions - and refined values for astronomical constants (such as the inclination of the ecliptic, solar parallax, and precession of the equinoxes) that improved on previous models. He also made advances in spherical astronomy and trigonometry: his work facilitated more accurate calculations for celestial coordinates and timekeeping. Because of the quality and precision of his data, his tables remained influential for centuries: they were used by later astronomers - including Western scientists - for computations long after his death. Ibn Yunus died in 1009 in Cairo (or Fustat) under the Fatimid Caliphate. Although much about his personal life remains obscure, his scientific legacy is clear: his systematic observations, rigorous calculations, and comprehensive tables mark him as one of the greatest astronomers of the medieval Islamic world. His work significantly influenced the development of astronomy and trigonometry in both the Islamic world and later Europe.

name: Henrietta Hill Swope
whatOpened: Showed that Cepheid variable stars can be used to measure distances across the universe - enabling determination of galaxy distances.
short: Henrietta Hill Swope (1902-1980) was an American astronomer whose precise measurements of variable stars (especially Cepheids) helped to calibrate cosmic distance scales - a key step toward understanding the size of the Milky Way and distances to other galaxies.
long: Henrietta Hill Swope was born on 26 October 1902 in St. Louis, Missouri. She studied mathematics at Barnard College and graduated in 1925, then went on to earn a master's degree in astronomy from Radcliffe College. In 1928 she began working at the Harvard College Observatory (HCO), focusing on photometry and the study of variable stars. During her time at Harvard (1928-1942), her observations contributed to the shift in understanding the 45 structure of the Milky Way - under her and her colleagues' studies, the galactic center was identified away from the Sun's position. By 1934 she had catalogued more than 1,000 variable stars. In 1936, she joined an expedition (Harvard-MIT) to observe a solar eclipse, using a 6 - inch telescope to photograph the solar corona; her participation demonstrates the breadth of her observational work. With the outbreak of World War II, Swope left Harvard and in 1942 joined the MIT Radiation Laboratory, later working as a mathematician for the U.S. Navy's Hydrographic Office on navigation - contributing her computational skills to wartime efforts. After the war, she resumed astronomy: from 1952 she worked as a research fellow at the Mount Wilson Observatory and Palomar Observatory in California (also associated with Carnegie Institution for Science), focussing on Cepheid variables. Her measurements of Cepheids in other galaxies - including using the Hale 200 - inch telescope - were instrumental in establishing extragalactic distance estimates. In 1967 she made a generous donation of US$ 650,000 to the Carnegie Institution to support the creation of an observatory in the Southern Hemisphere; this contribution enabled construction of a 40 - inch telescope at what became the Las Campanas Observatory in Chile, which was named Swope Telescope in her honor. Henrietta Hill Swope died on 24 November 1980 in Pasadena, California. Her work - precise photometry, variable - star catalogues, Cepheid studies - laid foundational stones for modern extragalactic astronomy. The Swope 46 Telescope and asteroid 2168 Swope bear her name, preserving her legacy through time.

name: Elmer Samuel Imes
whatOpened: Demonstrated that quantum theory applies to molecular rotational and vibrational energy states via infrared spectroscopy.
short: Elmer Samuel Imes was an American physicist whose pioneering high - resolution infrared spectroscopy of diatomic molecules (HCl, HBr, HF) provided early empirical verification of quantum theory and helped found the study of molecular structure via IR spectroscopy.
long: Elmer Samuel Imes was born on 12 October 1883 in Memphis, Tennessee, USA. He graduated in 1903 with a bachelor's degree in science from Fisk University, a historically Black university. After some years teaching physics and mathematics at historically Black institutions in the American South, he returned to Fisk and earned a master's degree before enrolling in graduate studies at University of Michigan. There, under 48 the supervision of Harrison Randall, he designed and built a series of high - resolution infrared (IR) spectrometers. In 1918 he earned his Ph.D. in physics - becoming the second African American (after much earlier trailblazers) to receive a doctorate in physics in the United States. His doctoral dissertation led to a landmark 1919 publication in the Astrophysical Journal, "Measurements on the Near - Infrared Absorption of Some Diatomic Gases," in which he reported the first precise IR absorption spectra of diatomic molecules such as hydrogen chloride (HCl), hydrogen bromide (HBr), and hydrogen fluoride (HF). These results demonstrated that both rotational and vibrational energy levels of molecules are quantized - a strong empirical support for then - emerging quantum theory. Moreover, his spectra allowed for the first accurate measurements of interatomic distances in molecules and provided early evidence for molecular isotopy (e.g., isotopes of chlorine based on absorption band structure). After earning his Ph.D., Imes moved to New York (1918) and worked in industry - as a research physicist and engineer for several firms - where he held four patents for instruments designed to measure magnetic and electric properties. In 1930, he returned to Fisk University, where he became Chairman of the Physics Department. Over the following decade until his death, he worked on developing the curriculum and mentoring a new generation of physicists. Elmer Samuel Imes died on 11 September 1941 in New York City.

name: Agnes Luise Wilhelmine Pockels
location: Venice (then Kingdom of Lombardy-Venetia, Austrian Empire) → later Brunswick (Braunschweig), Germany
whatOpened: Showed that liquid surface films change surface tension depending on contamination and area - foundational for surface science.
short: Agnes Pockels was a German self - taught chemist/physicist whose home experiments revealed how impurities and molecular films influence surface tension - pioneering the science of surfaces and interfaces.
long: Agnes Pockels was born on 14 February 1862 in Venice (then part of the Austrian Empire). In 1871 her family moved to Brunswick (Braunschweig), Germany, after her father's retirement from the military. Women at that time were not permitted to attend university, so although Agnes was interested in natural science from a young age, she never received formal higher - education in chemistry or physics. 51 Despite these limitations, she educated herself using physics textbooks borrowed from her younger brother (who studied physics), and began conducting experiments at home - motivated partly by everyday observations like washing dishes. Around 1882 she invented a simple experimental apparatus - a "sliding trough" (Schieberinne) - that allowed her to vary the water surface area and measure surface tension changes using a balance and a small disk floating on the surface. Using this device, Pockels demonstrated that adding minute amounts of impurities (e.g. oils or surfactants) to water drastically alters surface tension; when the surface area is compressed to a certain limit, the surface tension changes sharply - a phenomenon now crucial for understanding monomolecular films at interfaces. She computed that a single molecule occupies a minimum surface area in such a film - later referred to as the "Pockels point". In 1891 - unable to publish in German journals because she lacked institutional affiliation - she wrote to the prominent British physicist Lord Rayleigh describing her findings. Impressed, he forwarded her letter to the journal Nature, which published it under the title "Surface Tension." That marked the beginning of her recognition by the scientific community. Over subsequent years she published a series of articles on surface films, contamination effects, wetting phenomena, and measurements of interfacial tension in different systems. Though lacking formal lab affiliation, her work laid essential groundwork for what would become the field of surface chemistry / surface science. Later in life, after decades of her independent research, she received formal acknowledgment: in 1931 she was awarded the Laura - R. Leonard prize of the German Colloid Society, and in 1932 she became the first woman to receive an 52 honorary doctorate from the Technical University of Braunschweig. Agnes Pockels died on 21 November 1935 in Brunswick, Germany. Her sliding trough and monolayer film measurements paved the way for modern methods in colloid and surface science - the ancestors of contemporary instruments like the Langmuir trough.

name: Viktor Amazaspovich Ambartsumian
whatOpened: Showed that many stars form continuously in associations - stellar formation is ongoing, not a once - in - a - lifetime event.
short: Viktor Ambartsumian was a Soviet - Armenian astrophysicist who founded the school of theoretical astrophysics in the USSR and showed that star formation continues in clusters ("stellar associations"), revolutionizing our view of the galaxy's lifecycle.
long: Viktor Amazaspovich Ambartsumian was born on 18 September 1908 in Tbilisi (then Russian Empire). He studied physics and mathematics at what was then Leningrad State University. After finishing his studies, Ambartsumian became a graduate student at the Pulkovo Observatory, and by the early 1930s began lecturing and working as a researcher - eventually founding the first astrophysics chair in the Soviet Union. In the 1930s and 1940s, he developed theoretical frameworks linking the ultraviolet radiation of hot stars with surrounding interstellar gas, leading to advances in understanding of gaseous nebulae and star formation. In 1947 he proposed the concept of "stellar associations" - groups of young stars born together - and argued that many stars are formed continuously over time, not only in the early Universe. In 1946 Ambartsumian founded the Byurakan Astrophysical Observatory (BAO) near Yerevan, Armenia, becoming its first director, a position he held for decades. Under his leadership, BAO became a major center for astrophysical research. 55 Ambartsumian's research extended beyond star formation: he studied interstellar matter, radiation transfer in "dusty" media, dynamics of stellar systems, and non - stationary processes in galaxies - laying groundwork for theories of active galactic nuclei and extragalactic astronomy. He authored over 200 scientific papers and numerous books, including the influential textbook Theoretical Astrophysics, the first systematic manual on astrophysics in Russian. Ambartsumian died on 12 August 1996 at Byurakan Observatory. Throughout his career, he received many honors - from national awards of the Soviet Union and Armenia, to international recognition.

name: Eunice Newton Foote
whatOpened: Showed that carbon dioxide and water vapour absorb more heat than air - earliest experimental evidence linking atmospheric gases to warming.
short: Eunice Newton Foote was an American scientist whose 1856 experiments first demonstrated that carbon dioxide and water vapour trap heat more effectively than ordinary air - a foundational insight for what later became known as the greenhouse effect.
long: Eunice Newton Foote was born on 17 July 1819 in Goshen, Connecticut, USA. She received her early education at the Troy Female Seminary (now Emma Willard School), one of the first U.S. schools providing advanced science instruction to women. 58 Foote married lawyer Elisha Foote in 1841, and they settled in Seneca Falls, New York. In 1856 she conducted a series of experiments: using glass cylinders filled with different gases (carbon dioxide, moist air, dry air, hydrogen, oxygen), she exposed them to sunlight and measured temperature changes. She found that carbon dioxide warmed the most and retained heat the longest. From these results, she concluded that if Earth's atmosphere had higher CO ₂ levels, it would lead to higher temperatures. Her findings were presented on 23 August 1856 at the meeting of the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS), although - because of prevailing gender norms - she did not present them herself; the paper was read by Joseph Henry. Later that year, her paper "Circumstances affecting the heat of the sun's rays" was published in the American Journal of Science and Arts. Despite the significance of her results, Foote's work was largely overlooked for more than a century; the credit for the heat - absorbing properties of CO ₂ was historically attributed to John Tyndall, whose experiments appeared a few years later (1859). Foote died on 30 September 1888 in New York. Although limited by the experimental methods of her time (she did not identify infrared radiation or fully articulate the modern mechanism of greenhouse warming), her demonstration of heat - absorption by CO ₂ and water vapour is now recognized as a pioneering step in climate science.

name: Katsuko Saruhashi
whatOpened: Developed a method to measure carbon dioxide (and carbonic acid) in seawater - a foundation for modern ocean - chemistry studies.
short: Katsuko Saruhashi was a pioneering Japanese geochemist who created a standardized method (later called "Saruhashi's Table") for measuring carbonic acid/CO ₂ levels in seawater and conducted foundational research on radioactive fallout and ocean contamination, deeply influencing oceanography and environmental science.
long: Katsuko Saruhashi was born on 22 March 1920 in Tokyo, Japan. She studied at the Imperial Women's College of Science (now Toho University), graduating in 1943. After graduation, she joined the Geochemical Laboratory at the Meteorological Research Institute under Japan's Meteorological Agency. Beginning around 1950, she started investigations into the chemistry of seawater - at a time when CO ₂ in the ocean was not considered significant. To carry out her research, Saruhashi developed an analytical method - now known as "Saruhashi's Table" - that allowed oceanographers to estimate the concentration of carbonic acid in natural waters by measuring three parameters: temperature, pH, and chlorinity. This methodology became a global standard for decades in ocean - chemistry research. In the 1950s and 1960s she expanded her work: following nuclear tests (e.g., at Bikini Atoll), she analyzed seawater and rainwater for radioactive isotopes (like cesium - 137 and strontium - 90). Her findings showed how fallout spread through oceans - data that contributed to international awareness about environmental hazards of nuclear testing. 62 In recognition of her scientific achievements and pioneering role, in 1957 she became the first woman to earn a doctorate in chemistry from the University of Tokyo. Later, in 1980, she became the first woman elected to the Science Council of Japan. Beyond her research, Saruhashi was a strong advocate for women in science. In 1958 she founded the Society of Japanese Women Scientists to support female researchers. In 1981 she established the Saruhashi Prize - an annual award recognizing outstanding Japanese women scientists. Katsuko Saruhashi passed away on 29 September 2007 in Tokyo. Her work fundamentally shaped modern geochemistry and oceanography, and her advocacy helped open pathways for women in science in Japan and beyond.

name: Annie Scott Dill Maunder (née Russell)
whatOpened: Showed that sunspot emergence migrates from high latitudes toward the solar equator over the - year solar cycle - laying the foundation for understanding solar cycles.
short: Annie Maunder was an Irish - British astronomer and astrophotographer who, together with her husband, produced the first “butterfly diagram” of sunspot latitudes - a fundamental tool in solar physics - and documented the period of very low solar activity now known as the “Maunder Minimum.”
long: Annie Scott Dill Russell was born on 14 April 1868 in Strabane, County Tyrone, Ireland. She was educated in mathematics and, in 1886, gained a scholarship to Girton College, Cambridge, where she passed the Mathematical Tripos with honours in - ranking top of her class - though Cambridge at the time did not award degrees to women. In 1891 she began working at Royal Observatory, Greenwich (ROG) as one of the “lady computers,” assigned to the Solar Department. Her work involved photographing the Sun daily with a photoheliograph, measuring sunspot positions and areas from those images. In December 1895 she married Edward Walter Maunder, the head of the Solar Department. Because of regulations at the time restricting married women from public - service posts, she resigned from her paid position - but she and her husband continued collaborating on solar research voluntarily. Despite these obstacles, Annie remained deeply active in astronomy. She accompanied her husband and other astronomers on multiple solar - eclipse expeditions (including Norway 1896, India 1898, and others), where she used a camera of her own design to photograph the solar corona. On the India expedition, she captured a very long ray (a coronal streamer) using a wide - field camera - a notable observational achievement. By carefully cataloguing decades of sunspot observations, in 1904 Annie and Edward Maunder created the now - famous “butterfly diagram”: a plot showing how sunspots first appear at higher solar latitudes then drift toward the equator over each ~11 - year solar cycle. This diagram remains a foundational representation in solar physics, used to understand solar cycles and activity patterns. In 1907, Annie Maunder published a solo paper analysing sunspot data (collected 1889-1901), demonstrating east-west asymmetry in sunspot distributions - a significant scientific contribution in her own right. Beyond research, she also co - authored a popular astronomy book The Heavens and Their Story (1908) with her husband - the work included many of her solar photographs and was widely used to bring astronomy to a broader audience. In recognition of her contributions and despite earlier institutional barriers, in Annie Maunder became one of the first women elected as a Fellow of the Royal Astronomical Society (RAS). Annie Scott Dill Maunder died on 15 September 1947 in Wandsworth, London, aged 79. Despite the constraints of her era - both social and institutional - Annie Maunder’s meticulous observations, photographic records, and conceptual contributions significantly advanced solar astronomy, leaving a lasting legacy in how we understand the Sun’s activity and its cycles.

name: Mary Arizona “Zonia” Baber
location: Kansas Township, Edgar County, Illinois, USA
whatOpened: Introduced field - work and experiential learning as central to geography education.
short: Zonia Baber was an American geographer and educator who pioneered modern geography teaching by emphasizing field work and hands - on learning, rather than rote memorization.
long: Zonia Baber was born on 24 August 1862 in Kansas Township, Edgar County, Illinois. Early on, she pursued teacher training: after high school she attended a normal school to become a teacher. She began her teaching career in 1886 as the head (principal) of a private school for two years (1886-1888). Then she took a position at Cook County Normal School (which later became part of Chicago State University), where from to she served as head of the Geography Department. While teaching, she continued her own education, attending geology classes (among the first at that time open to women) and eventually earning a Bachelor of Science in 1904. In she moved to the Department of Education at the University of Chicago, where she worked as associate professor and head of geography and geology until 1921. Baber was a strong advocate for a different approach to teaching geography: she believed in learning through direct experience, field trips, observation and hands - on work rather than memorizing place names and facts from textbooks. To support this, in she even designed a special type of school desk equipped with trays and compartments to store maps, specimens and educational materials - facilitating practical work. In 1898 she co-founded the Chicago Geographical Society - a public - oriented society aimed at promoting geography and field - based learning. She served as its president (1900-1904) and remained active in the society for decades. Beyond her contributions to geography education, Baber was committed to social justice, peace, and equality. She was a suffragist and worked for women’s rights, racial equality, anti - imperialism and educational access. She was involved with the Women’s International League for Peace and Freedom (WILPF), and with the Chicago branch of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), among other organizations. Zonia Baber died on 10 January 1956 in East Lansing, Michigan, USA. Her approach - integrating fieldwork, geology, environmental understanding and social awareness into geography education - had a lasting impact. Many modern methods of teaching geography, emphasizing hands - on experience, environmental awareness, and global citizenship, draw on ideas she helped develop. Recent scholarship also highlights her work as an early example of geo - ethics: connecting geoscience, social justice, and environmental stewardship.

name: Isabella Helen Karle (née Lugoski)
whatOpened: Developed practical X - ray scattering methods that made determination of molecular crystal structures efficient and reliable.
short: Isabella Karle was an American physical chemist and crystallographer whose methods for analyzing X - ray diffraction data revolutionized structural chemistry - enabling scientists to determine 3D molecular structures rapidly and accurately.
long: Isabella Helen Lugoski was born on 2 December 1921 in Detroit, Michigan, USA. Her parents were immigrants from Poland: her father worked for the city’s transportation system, and her mother worked as a seamstress and later ran a small restaurant, where young Isabella helped manage accounts - an early sign of her affinity for numbers. After finishing public school (she advanced quickly, skipping two grades), she began her higher education at the University of Michigan on a full scholarship. She earned a B.S. in chemistry at age 19, then went on to obtain M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in physical chemistry - all by the age of 23. During her graduate studies she met her future husband and lifelong collaborator, Dr. Jerome Karle; they married in 1942. Shortly after, during World War II, she contributed to the Manhattan Project at the University of Chicago, where she worked on chemical methods to extract plutonium chloride from plutonium oxide mixtures. In 1946, Isabella and Jerome Karle joined the United States Naval Research Laboratory (NRL) in Washington, D.C., where she spent the bulk of her career - over six decades. There, she worked to solve the longstanding “phase problem” in X - ray crystallography. While her husband and colleague Herbert Hauptman developed the mathematical underpinnings (the “direct methods”), Isabella Karle translated those theories into practical, experimental protocols. Her “Symbolic Addition Procedure” and experimental X - ray scattering techniques allowed determination of the three - dimensional structures of complex molecules - from small organic compounds to biological molecules. Throughout her career she successfully elucidated numerous molecular structures, including toxins, natural products, antibiotics, and other biologically relevant molecules. By enabling fast and accurate structural determination, her work laid foundations for modern structural biology, materials science, pharmacology, and many branches of chemistry where knowing molecular structure is crucial. Isabella Karle retired in 2009 (together with her husband), after 63 years at NRL. She died on October at age in Arlington, Virginia, USA, due to complications from a brain tumour.

name: Charles Henry Turner
whatOpened: Showed that insects (bees, ants, cockroaches) can learn and adapt behaviour based on experience - a foundational insight for animal cognition studies.
short: Charles Henry Turner was an American zoologist and entomologist whose experiments proved that insects are capable of learning, remembering, and adapting their behavior - fundamentally challenging the view of insects as mere automatons.
long: Charles Henry Turner was born on 3 February 1867 in Cincinnati, Ohio. He completed high school in as valedictorian at Gaines High School, then enrolled at the University of Cincinnati, earning a B.S. in biology in and an M.S. in 1892. After his master’s degree, Turner worked briefly at the University of Cincinnati as an instructor, then from to taught biology at Clark College (now part of Clark Atlanta University) in Atlanta. In he earned a Ph.D. in zoology from the University of Chicago - likely the first African American to receive a Ph.D. in that field from the institution. Despite his advanced education and prolific output, Turner encountered racial barriers in academia. As a result, he spent much of his career teaching at high - school level. From until his retirement in he taught science at Sumner High School in St. Louis, Missouri. Parallel to teaching, Turner conducted pioneering research on insect behavior. Over his ~33 - year career he published more than research papers. He designed experimental apparatus (e.g. mazes for ants and cockroaches, colored disks and boxes for bees) and performed experiments demonstrating that insects can hear, navigate, learn by trial and error, and - for social insects like bees - respond to visual patterns and remember tasks. Notable findings: ● He produced early evidence that cockroaches could learn to avoid a dark chamber and retain the avoidance behavior under new conditions. ● He studied pattern recognition in honeybees, suggesting bees could discriminate visual signals rather than rely solely on scent. ● Through controlled experiments on ants, he provided evidence that their navigation and homing behaviour involved more than simple reflex - a key insight toward recognizing insect cognition. Turner also committed himself to civil rights and educational equality. He believed in the power of education to combat racism and dedicated much of his teaching and public writing to advocate for equal opportunities for African Americans. Charles Henry Turner died on 14 February 1923 in Chicago, Illinois. Despite systemic barriers, Turner’s work laid vital foundations for the study of animal cognition and behavioural biology, showing that even ostensibly “simple” creatures-like insects-are capable of learning and adaptation.

name: Nils Ivar Bohlin
whatOpened: Introduced the modern three - point safety belt - a simple, effective occupant - restraint system that became the standard in virtually every car worldwide.
short: Nils Bohlin (1920-2002) was a Swedish engineer whose design of the three - point seatbelt revolutionized automotive safety - the belt is credited with saving millions of lives since its adoption.
long: Nils Bohlin was born on 17 July 1920 in Härnösand, Sweden. He studied mechanical engineering and in began his career at the aircraft manufacturer Saab, where he worked on ejector seats and various aeronautical safety designs - experience that informed his later work in automotive safety. In 1958 Bohlin was hired by Volvo as the company’s first safety engineer. Within a year he developed a new occupant - restraint system for cars: a seatbelt combining a lap belt and a diagonal shoulder strap, anchored to the vehicle so that in case of sudden deceleration both upper and lower body are restrained. In 1959 Volvo became the first automaker to introduce Bohlin’s three - point belt as standard equipment in its cars (models like the P120 Amazon and PV544). The design was patented (U.S. Patent No. 3,043,625) in 1962. Volvo also released the patent so that all manufacturers could use the belt - contributing to widespread adoption and global road - safety improvements. Bohlin continued his work at Volvo, eventually leading the company’s research and development department. He also contributed to side - impact protection research and other automotive safety innovations. He retired in 1985. He died on 26 September 2002 at age 82.

name: Sophia (Sophie) Brahe
major: Astronomy / / Genealogy / Early chemistry / Medicine (self - taught)
whatOpened: Demonstrated that women could contribute to serious astronomical observation - assisted in planetary and lunar observations that became the foundation for more accurate celestial calculations.
short: Sophia Brahe was a Danish noblewoman who, despite social norms, studied astronomy, chemistry, and horticulture. She assisted her brother Tycho Brahe in his celestial observations, and later compiled a major genealogical work on Scandinavian noble families.
long: Sophia Brahe was born around 1556 (or possibly 1559) at Knutstorp estate in Scania (then part of Denmark-Norway), the youngest of ten children in the noble Brahe family. Her older brother was Tycho Brahe, who became a famous astronomer. Although their family expected more typical roles for nobility, Sophia - raised at home - received a broad private education and developed an interest in natural sciences. When she was a teenager, Sophia began assisting Tycho in his astronomical observations. She is known to have taken part in measuring the lunar eclipse of December 1573, and she visited his observatory many times. Although Tycho had initially discouraged her from seriously pursuing astronomy - saying it was too “complex for a woman” - Sophia persisted, teaching herself astronomy, translating Latin books into German for her own study, and gaining recognition from her brother for her competence. In 1579 she married the nobleman Otte Thott, owner of Eriksholm estate, and in gave birth to a son, named Tage. When her husband died in 1588, she took over management of the estate on behalf of her son - and during that time developed her interest in horticulture, medicine, and chemistry. She designed and maintained remarkable gardens around the estate and cultivated medicinal plants. Despite marriage and motherhood, Sophia continued her scientific interests: she maintained contact with Tycho and often visited his island observatory. Later, she began working on genealogy - compiling detailed records of Danish and Scandinavian noble families. Her genealogical manuscript, completed around 1626, spanned pages and remains an important source for Scandinavian noble genealogy. In her later years, Sophia’s scientific interests also included chemistry, medicine, and herbalism; she used her knowledge to produce medicines from her garden’s plants for the local community. She died in (or around that year) in Helsingør (Elsinore), Denmark. Although Sophia Brahe never received formal academic positions (women of her time were barred from universities), her life exemplifies the intellectual capacity of Renaissance women and demonstrates that serious scientific work was possible outside formal institutions. Her astronomical assistance, botanical and medicinal work, genealogical scholarship, and management of estates make her a remarkable polymath whose contributions have often been under - recognized.

name: Caroline Lucretia Herschel
whatOpened: Showed that women can be professional astronomers - discovered multiple comets and catalogued nebulae .
short: Caroline Herschel was a German - born British astronomer, recognized as the first professional female astronomer. She discovered eight comets, catalogued nebulae and star clusters, and made important contributions to stellar catalogues.
long: Caroline Lucretia Herschel was born on 16 March 1750 in Hanover, Germany. As a child she suffered typhus, which stunted her growth and left her physically frail - she never grew more than about 1.3 meters tall. Her mother expected her to do household work, but her father educated her privately in mathematics, music and literacy. In 1772 her brother William Herschel invited her to England; there she assisted him with household tasks but also studied mathematics and astronomy under his guidance. Initially she polished mirrors for telescopes and maintained observational logs. Over time, she began her own observations. Between 1783 and 1786 she discovered several nebulae and - on 1 August - became the first woman to discover a comet (later known as 35P/Herschel-Rigollet). Over the next few years she discovered a total of eight comets. Beyond comet - hunting, in 1798 she produced an updated index to the star catalogue of John Flamsteed, adding stars omitted previously - a major correction and expansion of known stellar data. After the death of her brother in 1822, she returned to Hanover and undertook the task of reorganising and cataloguing about 2,500 nebulae and star clusters discovered by them. In recognition of her work, in 1787 she was granted a salary by King George III as her brother’s assistant - making her the first woman formally paid for scientific work. In 1828, at age 78, she was awarded the Gold Medal of the Royal Astronomical Society for her catalogue work; she was later made honorary member. Caroline Herschel died on 9 January 1848 in Hanover at the age of 97. Her meticulous observations, cataloguing, and discoveries - especially as a woman in a male - dominated field - laid important groundwork for future generations of astronomers and helped expand humanity's knowledge of comets, nebulae and the structure of the night sky. Her legacy remains as a pioneer who broke gender barriers in science.

name: Mary Fairfax Somerville
whatOpened: Demonstrated that science and mathematical knowledge can be accessible, and unified many fields into a coherent view - laying groundwork for modern interdisciplinary science.
short: Mary Somerville was a Scottish polymath whose writings and translations made complex astronomy, physics, geography, and mathematics accessible - earning her the title “the queen of 19th - century science.”
long: Mary Fairfax was born on 26 December 1780 in Jedburgh, Scotland. As a child she received minimal formal schooling (girls’ education was limited at the time) - she was briefly sent to a girls’ boarding school in Musselburgh, but much of her learning came from self - study, with encouragement from her uncle who helped her with Latin. In 1804 she married Samuel Greig, a Russian - navy captain; after his death in she gained independence to pursue her mathematical interests. In she remarried Dr William Somerville, who supported her studies; this second marriage enabled her entry into scientific circles in Edinburgh and London, where she befriended eminent scientists of the day. Despite limited formal training, she mastered mathematics, physics, astronomy and geography through reading, correspondence, and study of original scientific works. Her first scientific paper, “On the Magnetizing Power of the More Refrangible Solar Rays,” was published in 1826. In 1831 she published The Mechanism of the Heavens, a translation and commentary on Pierre-Simon Laplace’s Mécanique céleste , making celestial mechanics accessible to English - speaking readers. Three years later came On the Connection of the Physical Sciences (1834), a work unifying astronomy, physics, geography, and meteorology - hugely influential and widely read; several editions were published during her lifetime. In this work she noted that anomalies in the orbit of Uranus might hint at another planet - a suggestion that helped inspire the eventual discovery of Neptune. In 1848 she published Physical Geography, the first English textbook on that subject, used widely in education for decades. Throughout her life, despite societal obstacles to women in science, she corresponded with leading scientists (e.g. John Herschel, Michael Faraday), contributed to public understanding of science, and was recognised for her clarity and breadth of knowledge. In 1835 she - along with Caroline Herschel - became one of the first women elected honorary members of the Royal Astronomical Society. Mary Somerville died on 29 November 1872 in Naples, Italy, at age 91. Her legacy includes founding a tradition of accessible scientific writing, promoting interdisciplinary science, breaking gender barriers in scientific communities - and inspiring generations of women scientists. The prestigious Somerville College, Oxford (founded 1879) bears her name.

name: Fanny Angelina Hesse (née Eilshemius)
whatOpened: Proposed use of agar as a solid culturing medium - enabling reliable microbial cultures for the first time.
short: Fanny Hesse was a pioneering figure in microbiology: she suggested using agar - a seaweed - derived gel she knew from cooking - instead of gelatin, making it possible to grow and study bacteria reliably for the first time.
long: Fanny Angelina Hesse was born on 22 June 1850 in New York City, USA. Later she grew up in Kearny, New Jersey. In she married German physician and microbiologist Walther Hesse, after which the couple relocated to Germany so Walther could work in the laboratory of Robert Koch. Though not a formally trained scientist, Fanny helped in the laboratory by preparing media, drawing microscopic observations, and supporting her husband’s research. In the hot summer of 1881, when gelatin used as a growth medium kept liquefying - ruining microbial cultures - Fanny suggested using agar - agar instead. She knew agar from childhood cooking traditions: it was used in jellies and desserts and remained firm even in warm weather. Agar had two key advantages over gelatin: it remained solid at incubation temperatures and resisted breakdown by bacterial enzymes - making it ideal for culturing bacteria. Her suggestion enabled Walther (and Koch) to successfully cultivate bacteria - including the tuberculosis bacillus - on solid media, transforming bacteriology from a largely qualitative science into one capable of producing reproducible, isolatable cultures. Beyond that, Fanny also created detailed scientific illustrations of bacterial colonies and maintained meticulous lab notes - contributions that supported early microbiological publications. Despite the huge impact of her idea, neither Fanny nor Walther Hesse received formal credit in Koch’s publications at the time; agar - based culture media became standard worldwide without her name becoming widely known. Fanny Hesse died on 1 December 1934.

name: Rosalba Giovanna Carriera
whatOpened: Popularized pastel as a serious medium for portraiture, transforming the status of pastels in European art.
short: Rosalba Carriera was a Venetian Rococo painter and miniature - portrait artist; she revolutionized European portrait painting by elevating pastel - previously used only for sketches - to a respected medium for distinguished portraits.
long: Rosalba Carriera was born on 7 October 1675 in Venice, to Andrea Carriera (a clerk) and Alba Foresti, a lace - maker. In her youth she helped with lace - making and decorative arts, a common female craft, but as the lace - industry demand declined, she turned to miniature painting - decorating snuff - box lids and small objects for tourists. She began painting miniatures on ivory rather than traditional vellum, giving her works a distinctive luminosity and helping them stand out. Her early success led to her acceptance in the prestigious art institution Accademia di San Luca in Rome in - a notable achievement for a woman at the time. In the early 18th century Carriera transitioned to pastel portraiture. She perfected techniques of soft shading, subtle coloring, and delicate rendering of fabrics and textures - qualities well suited to the growing Rococo aesthetic. In 1720, at the invitation of a prominent collector, she moved to Paris with her family. There she painted portraits of many high - ranking clients, including the youthful Louis XV of France. During this period, she became enormously fashionable among European nobility and aristocracy. Her reputation spread across Europe - she also worked in courts in Modena, Vienna (at the Habsburg imperial court), and had patrons from various European states. Despite fame, Carriera never married; she lived with her mother and sisters. After the death of her sister Giovanna (her longtime assistant) in and progressive failure of her eyesight in later years, her productivity declined. Rosalba Carriera died on 15 April 1757 in Venice. Her pastel portraits, miniatures, and stylistic innovations left a lasting mark on European art, and she remains celebrated as one of the most successful women artists of any era.

name: Dorothea Klumpke Roberts
whatOpened: She became the first woman in history to earn a Doctor of Science in astronomy (1893) and led measurement work at Paris Observatory, contributing to the huge international sky - mapping project Carte du Ciel - paving the way for modern stellar atlases and opening professional astronomy to women.
short: Dorothea Klumpke Roberts was an American-born astronomer who became the first woman ever to earn a doctorate in astronomy and headed the measurement bureau at Paris Observatory, participating in the major international effort to photograph the entire sky.
long: Dorothea Klumpke Roberts was born on August 9, 1861 in San Francisco, California. Her family moved to Europe for education, and she studied mathematics and astronomy at the Sorbonne, graduating in 1886. In 1893 she successfully defended her doctoral thesis L’étude des Anneaux de Saturne (A Study of the Rings of Saturn), thereby becoming the first woman in the world to obtain an advanced doctorate in astronomy (Doctor ès sciences). In 1887 she joined the Paris Observatory and soon became significantly involved in the international sky-survey project Carte du Ciel, which aimed to photograph and catalogue stars across the heavens. Because of her skills, she was appointed Director of the Observatory’s Bureau of Measurements (Bureau des Mesures), a position she held from 1892 to 1901, supervising a team, including women “computers,” responsible for measuring star positions on photographic plates. In 1896 she participated in an expedition to observe a total solar eclipse in Norway. In 1899 she was selected by French astronomers to ascend in a hot-air balloon (Le Centaure) to observe the anticipated Leonid meteor shower, making her the first woman to conduct astronomical observations from a balloon. In 1901 she married astronomer Isaac Roberts, a pioneer in astrophotography, and moved to England. After his death in 1904, she inherited his equipment and estate and took over the work of cataloguing nebulous regions. She completed and published in 1929 The Isaac Roberts Atlas of 52 Regions, a Guide to William Herschel’s Fields of Nebulosity, a major reference for faint nebulous objects. Beyond her astronomical research, Dorothea gave back to the scientific community: she established endowments that eventually led to the creation of the Klumpke-Roberts Award, a prize recognizing contributions to the public understanding and appreciation of astronomy. Dorothea Klumpke Roberts passed away on October 5, 1942 in San Francisco. Her life opened a door for women in science at a time when such achievements were extremely rare.
